responseSet.title: Professor Carlo Lorenzo Cazzullo 30 January 1915 – 04 May 2010
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Professor Carlo Lorenzo Cazzullo 30 January 1915 – 04 May 2010

Giuseppe Spoto MRCPsych MA

Giuseppe Spoto

Giuseppe Spoto  

Professor Carlo Lorenzo Cazzullo, Emeritus Professor of Psychiatry at the University of Milan, died on 4 May 2010. He was 95 years old. He was the Father of Italian Psychiatry and the first Professor of Psychiatry in Italy, as distinct from Neurology. Before his appointment in 1959, there were no separate Chairs of Psychiatry in Italy, the only Professorships recognized in Italian Universities being Professorships of Nervous and Mental Diseases. Psychiatry was then only a complementary subject in the undergraduate medical curriculum.

After Professor Cazzullo's appointment, Psychiatry also became officially recognized as a separate medical speciality. It was later to become a core subject by law in 1976. The relevant legislation known as Legge no. 238 (28 April 1976) bears his name. Professor Cazzullo was responsible for turning psychiatry into a serious academic subject and also for the sweeping changes to the care of the mentally ill that were to follow.

responseSet.title: Bion today
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion today

Tom Russ

Tom Russ

Edinburgh, EH16 4SA

Tom Russ  

Chris, Mawson, , Routledge (New Library of Psychoanalysis), London, 2010, 464 pp., £22.99 (paperback)

Bion is often thought to be confusing, sometimes wilfully so, but this excellent book from the New Library of Psychoanalysis series goes a long way to make the relevance of his work clear. My interest in Bion stems from my general psychiatric training and his work on the psychotic and non-psychotic personalities (Bion, 1967a) but, in the field of dementia, many behaviours become understandable when considered in the light of his theories. When verbal communication diminishes, communication by projection or projective identification becomes increasingly prominent and the ability of care workers to contain painful emotional states becomes increasingly important.

The structure of the book will be familiar from ‘Melanie Klein today’ (Bott Spillius, 1988a, 1988b) with ‘mainly conceptual’ and ‘mainly clinical’ sections and additional parts entitled ‘aestheic’, ‘group mentality’ and ‘later Bion’. The majority of the book originated in the ‘Bion today’ conference which took place in 2005.

Chris Mawson, the volume's editor, effortlessly sets the scene in the outstanding first chapter, providing a coherence to the collected chapters which follow, not all of which are newly written. Edna O'Shaughnessy's companion introductory chapter selects, as the distinctive element of Bion's work, ‘the epistemophilic impulse … which as the “K link” was given equal placing by Bion with the relations of Love (L) and Hostility (H)’ (p. 17).

The conceptual section begins a re-consideration of the container-contained relationship (Bion, 1967c, 1970) which has been readily absorbed into day-to-day practice in a variety of disciplines as a useful metaphor, but often without giving credit to its originator.

Ron Britton's chapter in the conceptual section compares Bion's innovations in psychoanalysis to the development of quantum physics and specifically refers to Werner Heisenberg's famous ‘uncertainty principle’ (Heisenberg, 1927). It is particularly interesting to remember that Freud was developing his theories at the same time as the ‘destabilisation of classical physics’ (p. 72). Heisenberg's theory posits that it is impossible simultaneously to measure precisely both a particle's position and its momentum. The implication that the act of observing, in order to measure, alters the thing observed has been eagerly adopted in many fields as a metaphor for the impossibility of an objective observer and, therefore, the scientific ‘view from nowhere’ (Nagel, 1986).

responseSet.title: Work discussion: Learning from reflective practice in work with children and families
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Work discussion: Learning from reflective practice in work with children and families

Jean Murray

Jean Murray

Maryville Park
Belfast, Northern Ireland

Jean Murray  

Margaret Rustin and Jonathan Bradley, London, Karnac Books, 2008, 309 pp., £19.99 (paperback)

This edited volume on Work discussion was greeted by me with a mixture of relief and excitement. I was about to begin facilitating a work discussion seminar in a mental health setting and was finding it difficult to locate papers in the literature accessible to a group of seminar participants. So having eleven papers on work discussion gathered between the covers of one book felt like a godsend. However, this rather naïve reassurance soon gave way to an appreciation of the very real difficulties inherent in the work discussion task, which are ably brought to life in Margaret Rustin and Jonathan Bradley's book.

In the series editor's Preface to this volume, Margot Waddell spells out the very distinctive methodology that characterizes work discussion groups in mental health: that is, a methodology that is based upon learning from experience. This does not in itself seem either profound or original as the benefits of experiential learning have long been the clarion cry of educationalists. However, any teacher knows just how difficult it is to teach experientially and anyone who has facilitated a work discussion group knows too well how difficult it is to train professionals in a discipline that prizes the capacity not to know above knowing. The difficulty of working in this way is taken up by Trudy Klauber in her Foreword to the book and she gives very common examples of the ordinary reactions of participants to work discussion seminars, which have a ring of truth. The fact of the matter is that work discussion, with its emphasis on non-didactic learning, fuels negative emotions and anxieties as participants try to manage their anxieties regarding the participant observation task in hand, a task for which they feel unprepared. The difficulty of participating in a work discussion group is exacerbated by the fact that such groups tend to be made up of otherwise well-qualified professionals who are constantly put in a situation where knowing what to do rather than what it feels like to do something is all important. Klauber's Foreword is a useful preparation for the uninitiated seminar leader who requires skill in identifying and handling such anxieties without turning the work discussion group into a therapy group. Her emphasis on containment, and the practice of active listening and responding, rather than reacting, offers a framework to facilitate life-changing experiences for group participants which can and do alleviate stress at work.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

120 Belsize Lane
London, NW3 5BA

Alessandra Lemma  

One of the latest government policy documents, No health without mental health (2011), aptly reminds us not only that mind and body are closely related but that mental health is as vital as physical health. Investment in developing high quality services to help individuals with mental health problems should reflect this parity. The spirit of this document is commendable. However, despite its noble aspirations, we cannot help but be gravely concerned by the financial recession in which such ambitions will need to be realized. The efficiency savings that the NHS will have to make over the coming years are considerable and there are real risks to a range of mental health services, perhaps particularly to those offering psychoanalytic interventions. This is not just a potential threat. It's a reality: we know that in some services psychotherapy posts have already been cut or downgraded, and that long-established services are being decommissioned and closed down (e.g. the Cassel Families Unit).

The Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) programme has now embraced brief dynamic therapy as one of the therapies it provides, but this welcome development appears to be contributing to the decommissioning of psychotherapy departments in some parts of the country. Brief dynamic therapy should not be seen as a replacement for long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy: it should form part of a range of approaches provided so that we can meet the diverse needs of our patients.

Against this all-too-sobering backdrop the papers in this issue of the Journal serve to remind us of the vital contribution that clinicians informed by psychoanalytic thinking can make to helping individuals whose difficulties are complex and who most typically present for help in the public health sector (see Jones and Miller, Gloger, Wood, this issue). Complexity is hard to define. As some of the papers in this issue illustrate, it cannot be thought of solely as the property of an individual patient, but it is also, always, the property of a particular human system: the public sector organizations in which we work. In responding to complexity it then becomes essential to focus on clinician and service-based factors.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma  

A diagnosis of a complex learning disability, including autism or Asperger's, can have a profound impact on the individual and their family. Autistic Spectrum Disorders (ASD) are complex and varied conditions nowadays regarded as being multiply determined. The early psychogenic accounts of autism have been replaced by far more sophisticated models that integrate genetic and environmental determinants so as to understand what is regarded by some as a disorder of intersubjectivity (Hobson, 1993 and this issue). By their very nature ASDs present the clinician with a profound challenge: how to communicate with an individual who appears to lack curiosity and a desire for interaction with other people (Alvarez & Reid, 1999).

The three papers on ASDs in this special issue are written by clinicians working at the Tavistock Clinic (Hobson, Rhode, Urwin), which has a long and distinguished history of approaching autistic states from both psychoanalytic and developmental perspectives and of providing multidisciplinary services for this group of patients and their families. The Autism Workshop at the Tavistock Clinic, founded by Sue Reid in 1986 and later co-convened with Anne Alvarez, has originated a great deal of thinking in this area (Alvarez, 1992; Alvarez & Reid, 1999). The papers in this issue draw from these rich foundations, but also evidence recent developments in theory and clinical applications.

Peter Hobson sets the scene in a clear and thought-provoking overview of psychoanalytic contributions to autism, inviting us to engage in interdisciplinary dialogues, which he regards as essential to understanding ASDs and to developing effective interventions. The papers by Cathy Urwin and Maria Rhode ease us into the consulting room, with vivid and moving illustrations of work with children with ASDs, which remind us of the way clinicians have to struggle to stay connected with the ‘limited or distorted intersubjective experience’ (Hobson, this issue) of the autistic child or adult.

responseSet.title: Patients' experiences of forensic psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patients' experiences of forensic psychotherapy

Jessica Yakeley and Heather Wood

Heather Wood


London,

Heather Wood  

forensic psychotherapy, patient experience, goals and aims of therapy

In forensic psychotherapy there may be competing goals and agendas, influenced respectively by concerns about mental health and well-being, risk reduction and psychodynamically-significant change. There has, to date, been no published study of the goals and concerns of forensic patients undergoing therapy. Semi-structured interviews with ten patients considered by their therapists as having gained from forensic psychotherapy were analysed thematically. Results indicate the importance to patients of trust and acceptance in the therapeutic relationship, and changes in the domains of perception of self and interpersonal functioning as well as changes in problematic behaviours. These findings have implications for the choice of measures in systematic outcome research.

responseSet.title: The snows of yesteryear?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The snows of yesteryear?

Malcolm Allen

Malcolm Allen


London, UK

Malcolm Allen  

I can still remember the sense of shocked disbelief I had, some 13 years ago, when I was first told anecdotally that psychoanalysts generally considered homosexuality to be a disorder or even a perversion. At that point I had had no contact with psychoanalysis or psychoanalytic psychotherapy as a clinical practice. My relationship with psychoanalytic thought, other than through popular culture, was as a philosophy undergraduate studying philosophy of mind and later brushing across the Lacanian version that held sway in what we called film theory. For all my unfamiliarity, I had always associated psychoanalysis with the most forward-thinking, sophisticated and insightful enquiry into the human condition, especially the area of sexuality.

Around the same time in 1998, I attended the Inaugural Annual Lecture of the (then) British Confederation of Psychotherapists (BCP), later to become the British Psychoanalytic Council (BPC), given by the philosopher Richard Wollheim. It was he who had persuaded me, a fully paid-up empiricist, that Freud had successfully added the unconscious to the inventory of the human mind. Wollheim began with the same theme in the lecture, and then, following an elaboration of how things become unconscious, moved to another crucial innovation of Freud's thought, the concept of a ‘developmental story’. Conceding that, for Freud, the developmental story culminates in a heterosexual personality, Wollheim was adamant there was no justifiable shift from the notion of a developmental trajectory to something that is normative and, concomitantly, something that is more pathological. This was a relief, although I remember feeling that this welcome assertion had not entirely waved the problem away. There does seem to be something intrinsically teleological about a ‘developmental story’, with an Ought wrapped around an Is.

I had no inkling at the time I would become chief executive of the BPC eight years later, but when I was appointed I had a vague sense that there was important unfinished business here. It took time to untangle the knotted history as well as the no less complex realities of the present around this issue. The most conspicuous thing by far was the retreat into a Trappist silence within the psychoanalytic community on the subject, and by extension on the whole area of sexuality itself. The rampantly homophobic literature of earlier decades had tended to fade away during the 1990s, but little alternative theorization had taken its place. Most BPC member institutions had published generic equal opportunities statements.

responseSet.title: Emotional life of autistic spectrum children: What do we want from child psychotherapy treatment?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emotional life of autistic spectrum children: What do we want from child psychotherapy treatment?

Cathy Urwin

Cathy Urwin

London,

Cathy Urwin  

Autistic Spectrum Disorder, hopes and expectations for treatment approach, autistic object, resilience

This paper describes an ongoing evaluation of child psychotherapy with seven children with Autistic Spectrum Disorder (ASD). The assessment and evaluation model used involves parents and enhances shared understanding of each child's experience and characteristics, respecting individual strengths and limitations. Treatment aims include understanding the children's phenomenological experience and enhancing emotional regulation, to produce positive benefits for family life and the children's wellbeing. Common characteristics are identifiable despite differences between the children. Modifications in psychotherapy technique are illustrated with three cases. Nevertheless, the emerging phantasy life of these children shows commonalities with that of non-ASD children, further justifying the appropriateness of a psychoanalytic approach. The importance of these children's relationships with their psychotherapists and parents’ involvement in supporting the psychotherapy are stressed.

Experimenting with groups in a locked general psychiatry ward

John Steiner and Robert Harland

Robert Harland

Shirland Road, Maida Vale
London, UK

Denmark Hill
London, UK

Robert Harland  

The groups were discussed in terms of the role the mental hospital can play as a container of psychosis. The anxieties and concerns brought by the patients in the groups showed a high degree of disturbance with threats of disintegration and chaos prominent. Madness, violence, inappropriate sexual behaviour, and deprivation were brought in abundance and the staff struggled to deal with this by providing a containment based on attempts to understand. Although containment was far from successful the groups were valued by patients and staff alike and contributed to a more humane atmosphere where patient's concerns were given time and attention.

responseSet.title: The problem with psychoanalytic psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The problem with psychoanalytic psychotherapy

Paul Foster

Paul Foster

Burnt Oak, Broadway, Edgware
London,

Paul Foster  

Kirsty Hall, Nicola Godwin and Iain Snell, London, Karnac Books, 2010, 96 pp., £12.99 (paperback)

This is a small book with a large purpose, namely to describe the development of psychoanalytic theory from Freud to the present day, to highlight the limitations of psychoanalytic investigation and to discuss its effectiveness in comparison with non-analytic therapies.

The opening chapters point out inherent problems in the way that psychoanalytic discourse is formulated. The authors point out that the relationship of patient and therapist engaged in the process of psychodynamic narrative understanding make it a therapy not readily reducible to treatment manuals or NICE guidelines. However, there have been examples where therapy which is based on psychoanalytic principles is manualized making it possible to train consistently in the particular therapy. This also means that therapists can be assessed for adherence to the manual which is valuable in conducting research studies (Bateman & Fonagy, 2004; Luborsky, 2000).

They refer to ‘The question of lay analysis’ (Freud, 1926), which summarizes for the public his ideas concerning analytic therapy and go on to pose questions such as: Why are certain thoughts shameful and difficult to share? Is analysis simply based on suggestion and does it differ from a confessional? What is the nature of the sexual impulses of children and the role of trauma in neurosis? What is the difference between health and neurosis? Why does analysis take so long? How do we know when the correct interpretation has been given? These issues are developed into a discussion of Freud's early work with hypnosis, his theory of dreams, infantile sexuality and his later structural model of the mind.

The next chapter begins with Jung. It illustrates his wide interests in spirituality and religion while making it clear that he regarded himself as a ‘natural scientist’. The contributions of Klein, Winnicott, Fairbairn, Bowlby and Bion, object relations theorists, who were concerned in various ways with reciprocal mother infant–relatedness are discussed here followed by an examination of how much these practitioners developed a simple description and how much an explanatory structure of the mind.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jeremy Clarke and Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma



Alessandra Lemma  

An uneasy silence has prevailed for the most part on the subject of homosexuality within psychoanalytic psychotherapy. This special edition breaks that silence and does so in a particular way. Our selection of authors who write both with and without allegiance to psychoanalytic thinking is intended not only to open up a real dialogue but also to signal something more. The formation, three decades ago, of the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the NHS happened in the same year, 1981, that AIDS was discovered (Shilts, 1987). The global estimate of the death toll from AIDS stands at 30 million people (UNAIDS, 2010). At the other end of the actuarial spectrum, modern reproductive technology during this period has transformed the opportunities for lesbians and gay men to start their own families (Tasker, 2002). For psychoanalytic clinicians working in the NHS, then, the almost complete silence of our journal and our mainstream literature on how gay and lesbian people have dealt with these and other life and death issues, as well as with the ordinary realities of sustaining their relationships and work and mental well-being against a backdrop of social prejudice and institutionalized homophobia, including in our health services, demands an explanation.

Today, despite the increased acceptance of sexual diversity, the recent findings from a large-scale survey of over 6,000 lesbian and bi-sexual women indicate a disturbing level of distress. One in five respondents reported having deliberately harmed themselves in the last year, compared to one in 250 people in the general population. One in five report an eating disorder. One in twenty have attempted suicide, with an alarming 16% of those under the age of 20 attempting suicide (Hunt & Fish, 2008). If we reflect on these stark facts of life, juxtaposed with the near-silence of psychoanalysis, we cannot turn a blind eye to our role over many decades in pathologizing homosexuality. If we are to offer help to this population, we must put our house in order (King, 2011 and Allen, 2011; this edition).

responseSet.title: Under the skin. A psychoanalytic study of body modification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Under the skin. A psychoanalytic study of body modification

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter


London, UK

Joan Schachter  

Alessandra Lemma, London, Routledge, 2010, 205 pp., £21.99 (paperback)

Alessandra Lemma has written an interesting and timely book about the important subject of body modification; forms of which have become increasingly socially acceptable. She draws on her extensive experience in and out of the consulting room to formulate her psychoanalytic understandings of the motivations and meanings for the individual who compulsively engages in modes of changing the surface of their body through the use of tattoos, piercings or cosmetic surgery. Lemma begins the book with a vignette of a 15-year-old boy in a young offenders' institution, whose body was covered in tattoos. Initially she felt assaulted by his appearance; subsequently in the course of therapy she came to know about a terrified young man, serving a sentence for manslaughter, who had grown up in a violent and abusive home. From early adolescence he had used tattoos to attempt to (re)claim his body as his own from an intrusive mother. This experience powerfully conveyed ‘the psychic importance of the body and the ways in which internal fragmentation and hatred can be managed through changing the body's appearance’ (p. 2).

Lemma proposes that the more compulsive and extensive forms of body modification reflect a difficulty in integrating the ‘basic’ fact of life – that we cannot give birth to ourselves: ‘the body is living testament to our interrelatedness’. She outlines three dominant unconscious phantasies which she has found to be present in such individuals: the reclaiming phantasy – rescuing the self from an alien presence felt to reside inside the body, the self-made phantasy – expresses an envious attack on the object, and the perfect match phantasy – creating an ideal body that will guarantee the other's love and desire (p. 5). She suggests that these phantasies function to maintain the psychic equilibrium of these individuals. However, as she points out, they are frequently present in the same individual at different moments in the therapeutic process, and thus are not diagnostically useful, rather they emphasize different elements of the underlying narcissistic disturbance. This also alerts us to the danger of confusing description with explanation, which I think is greater when the economic and temporal dimensions of metapsychology are not considered. In her particular focus on object relations Lemma runs the risk of overlooking the importance of the economic dimension in the regulation of anxiety, affects and desire, derived from the drives, and of the role of temporality in the structuring and functioning in the unconscious of unconscious phantasies.

[[RunningHead]]

Cognitive behavioural therapy and family intervention for psychosis – evidence-based but unavailable? The next steps

Elizabeth Kuipers

Elizabeth Kuipers

London, UK

Elizabeth Kuipers  

National Institute for Clinical Excellence updated guidelines for schizophrenia (2009) recommend two psychological treatments – cognitive behavioural therapy for psychosis (CBTp) and family intervention for psychosis (FI). Despite these recommendations being in place for nearly a decade, implementation problems remain, particularly for FI. It is argued that these problems can be overcome, if services prioritise improving access to psychological therapies for psychosis, and that carers in particular need their own services to be developed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Treatment of self-harm in a learning-disabled woman using a combination of psychoanalytic and mentalization-based psychotherapy

Brian Turton

Brian Turton

London, UK

Brian Turton  

learning disability, self-harm, mentalization-based treatment, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, severe mental health problems

Learning disability is a debated term that nevertheless refers to a group of people who may at times need psychological intervention. This paper reviews the meanings given to this group of people and the impact on the parents and the child when the child is known to have a learning disability. Psychotherapeutic work with a patient with severe mental health difficulties on an in-patient unit is described. The theoretical work that supported the treatment was derived from two divergent approaches: psychoanalytic and mentalization-based. These were used at different points during treatment and the paper aims to demonstrate how this way of working can be of use with this type of presentation.

responseSet.title: Bion's therapeutic applications
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion's therapeutic applications

Gavin Ivey

Gavin Ivey

Melbourne, Australia

Gavin Ivey  

Bion, Projective identification, Truth, Containment, Thinking

Wilfred Bion is regarded as a psychoanalytic purist and his austere portrayal of the analytic aim and attitude is often considered to make impossible demands on patient and analyst alike. Not surprisingly, the applicability of Bion's theory and recommended practice to once-weekly psychotherapeutic work is often questioned. Bion is thus ambivalently regarded by psychotherapists as the embodiment of an analytic ideal, whose developmental theories are important, but whose practical utility is doubted, especially in the context of the typical therapeutic setting. This paper seeks to challenge these assumptions by presenting the once-weekly therapy of a woman with attenuated mentalizing capacity and a tendency to destructive acting out. The therapy was guided by the application of central concepts, models and ‘technical’ principles emerging from my understanding of Bion's work. The aim of the paper is to demonstrate that Bion's utility is not confined to formal psychoanalytic settings and that his work may be usefully applied in more modest psychotherapy contexts.

responseSet.title: Discussion paper of Carine Minne's paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion paper of Carine Minne's paper

Leslie Sohn

Leslie Sohn

Crowthorne, UK

Leslie Sohn  

This meeting was designed to remember and celebrate the life and clinical preoccupation of Richard Lucas, who was very involved in the psychotic elements in his patients, be it the straightforward presentation of a psychotic illness or, more significantly, the role of psychotic processes, either manifestly or unconsciously. He was particularly interested in the role of psychotic defences and how these psychical mechanisms can interfere with the treatments provided. Such defences can be overt such as when the patient says, ‘there's nothing wrong with me’ and refuses treatment, despite the obvious reasons for their admissions to a psychiatric hospital. Patients can also present with a variety of rationalizations that lead them to find treatment invasive or for whom any psychoanalytic enquiry of no interest.

The two patients discussed in Dr Minne's paper, despite the outcome in the young woman, show quite clearly their availability to our understanding of them and their therapeutic accessibility. This work would have been of considerable interest to Dr Lucas's mind and therapeutic attitude.

An important question is why there exists a gap between our understanding of our patients and theirs? This gap is greatest at the time of ‘acting out’ leading to their admission to a high security hospital. How strong is the psychotic process that made the patient unaware that he or she could take the life of another as opposed to just feel like killing them? What is happening in the mind of an individual that permits such enactment? Is it enough to look at the history of our patients to provide an explanation?

I don't minimize the significance of these individuals' traumatic histories, which hardly encourage normality or even neurotic patterns of behaviour. What happens to the developing person who ends up in a high security hospital, where they re-enact within the treatment system an attitude in living so reminiscent of the index offence? In other words, are there aggressive attitudes that permit such enactment from an original endogenous deficit? Alternatively, over the traumatic growing up years, does the accumulation of their behavioural patterns guarantee an absence of a mental structure that could protect them and their victims from such enactments? How will knowing or understanding change the contents of their minds?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Off the couch: Contemporary psychoanalytic approaches

Walter Gibson

Walter Gibson

Bradford,

Walter Gibson  

A. Lemma and M. Patrick, Routledge, 2010, 240 pp., £20.69 (paperback)

This volume appears as an accomplished addition to the project embraced by Alessandra Lemma, Matthew Patrick and the BPC itself to extend and realize the potential of psychoanalytic applications in the public sphere. Looking outwards, away from the couch, the ambition is clearly not only to publicize the relevant work being done in public institutions, but to challenge the perception of critics of the analytic modality as lacking both evidence base and relevance.

Addressing practitioners looking inward, there is also an aim to provide evidence of the relevance and need to address demands for accountability and the need to innovate and engage with the whole range of psychosocial, health, policy and political concerns which form the wider context within which clinical practice is claimed to be held to account in the marketized NHS. The volume takes its place among the remarkable achievements in securing a future for psychodynamic work in the NHS through a growing body of work including the core competencies allowing entry to the IAPT service and the recognition of psychodynamic relevance in depression by Alessandra Lemma and her colleagues.

The work presented is confident applied psychoanalytic understanding and practice, often informed and focused by complementary observation and research, including manualized treatments (Mothers and Babies Service). Applied clinical work covers suicide prevention, mothers and babies in prison, working with parents and under fives, traumatized adolescents, young black people, panic disorder, medically symptomatic patients, psychiatry and forensic work. This developmental and cultural range and depth is further enhanced by a chapter from John Alderdice showing how psychoanalytic insight can help to find a way to think and facilitate movement in situations of profound external and political complexity and conflict. Andrew Cooper and Julian Lousada take on the task of facing the conventional hierarchy and generational development of psychoanalytic authority with a challenge to face a changing world and locus of authority and power.

responseSet.title: The groups book. Psychoanalytic group therapy, principles and practice including the groups manual. A treatment manual with clinical vignettes/Difficult topics in group psychotherapy: My journey from shame to courage
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The groups book. Psychoanalytic group therapy, principles and practice including the groups manual. A treatment manual with clinical vignettes/Difficult topics in group psychotherapy: My journey from shame to courage

Debra Nash

Debra Nash


Debra Nash  

Caroline Garland, Tavistock Clinic Series, London, Karnac Books, 2010, 412 pp., £28.49 (paperback)

Jerome S. Gans, New International Library of Group Analysis, London, Karnac Books, 2010, 232 pp., £21.84

Both of these books about psychoanalytic group psychotherapy have as their focus the slow-open group and would be most relevant to those conducting such groups. What particularly commends them is that they have much to offer a wider audience. The groups book comprehensively and persuasively brings together the principles and practice of group analytic psychotherapy, but it also sets out the wider group context as the framework within which small group therapy can be understood. It would offer further depth and breadth of understanding to anyone interested in group life more generally. Difficult topics in group psychotherapy is as much about the development of the therapist as it is about the group. It is compelling and refreshing. It would be of interest to group and individual therapists. Both books would be worth reading for their clinical examples alone: these are rich in content and vividly portray the life of the therapy group.

At the heart of The groups book is the understanding that the major task for all human beings is to remain an individual capable of independent thought, while also being able to contribute to the ‘task’ of the group, whatever that may be. The editor's assertion is that well-functioning working groups can tackle issues that are beyond the isolated individual's grasp. However, to create a well-functioning group is a skilled job. We are told that this book is about the skills necessary to do this difficult job well, and that the wider hope is to increase the reader's understanding of group processes. It does this comprehensively and with clarity. Throughout, there is effective marrying of simplicity - referred to as ‘putting heads together’ and complexity.

The first three chapters are written by Garland who is also the editor. In Introduction: Groups and grouping, the wider group context is laid out. We are fixed firmly in the world of dictators, leaders, families, groups, lighthouse keepers, cults and mobs. The next two chapters, What is psychoanalytic about group therapy? and How does a psychoanalytic group work? bring us back into the world of the small group. She sets out to look at what psychoanalytic treatment and group treatment might have in common, and how they differ.

[[RunningHead]]

The initial psychotherapy interview: A gay man seeks treatment

Bernard Ratigan

Bernard Ratigan


Leicester, UK

Bernard Ratigan  

Charles Silverstein, Amsterdam, Elsevier, 2011, Available as an e-book or print on demand through either http://www.elsevierdirect.com/product.jsp?isbn = 9780123851468 or http://www.amazon.com/Initial-Psychotherapy-Interview-Treatment-Elsevier/dp/0123851467/ref = sr_1_15?ie = UTF8&qid = 1299609542&sr = 8-15

One of the challenges for the guardians of any body of knowledge/ideology/religion is what to do when there are changes that challenge the dominant paradigm. Psychoanalysis and homosexuality have had a difficult relationship. Yet, from the 1970s, certainly in North America, attitudes have begun to change. The UK still has a way to go. Much of Freud's radicalism about sexuality has been tamed, perhaps in an unconscious attempt to appear respectable. Silverstein's book will be an eye-opener to some in the UK. Here are confident gay and lesbian clinicians, including psychoanalysts, talking about non-pathologizing work with lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender (LGBT) patients. Almost all of the contributors write from a psychoanalytic or psychodynamic perspective.

responseSet.title: Emerging as a Personality Disorder Service
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emerging as a Personality Disorder Service

Barry Jones and Steve Miller

Steve Miller


Kent, UK


London, UK

Steve Miller  

personality disorder, mentalization, un heimlich, creative apperception

Personality Disorder Services working along psychotherapeutic lines are gaining appeal with Health Care Commissioners following the publication of the NICE guidelines for Borderline & Antisocial Personality Disorders and the emerging successful application of psychoanalytically-derived approaches.

Establishing a new Personality Disorder Service is akin to constructing a ‘home’ within which the therapeutic work with patients can develop. As clinicians, we are required to question what facets are important to the development of this new home, and to further to endure the unfamiliar state of being ‘not at home’ while the identity of the service is established.

We address how this process parallels the task facing the patient as they struggle to establish their own identity. We also highlight some important tenets in the work of securing a robust identity for both the service and the patient suffering from a personality disorder.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy with an adolescent boy who refused medical treatment

Simonetta Adamo and Wanda de Cristofaro

Wanda de Cristofaro

Bicocca
Milan, Italy


Naples, Italy

Wanda de Cristofaro  

adolescence, paediatric hospital, refusal of treatment, skin, body, child psychotherapy

In this paper we describe psychotherapeutic work carried out in a paediatric hospital to illustrate the important contribution of a psychoanalytically-oriented approach in a hospital context. The work concerns an adolescent boy who had undergone several surgical operations with the result that some of his natural orifices were obstructed and artificial ones had to be created. He was referred to us because he refused pharmacological and rehabilitative treatment necessary prior to further surgery and his eventual recovery. The boy's experience of loss of control of his life and the more primitive anxieties of not feeling safely contained in his physical and psychic skin emerged and were worked through in the psychotherapeutic relationship. The parallel work with medical staff reduced the risk of splitting and acting out by the patient and professionals. The approach adopted enabled this boy to resume his medical treatment and his developmental process.

[[RunningHead]]

The secluded minds of violent patients

Carine Minne

Carine Minne

London, UK

Carine Minne  

In this paper, I will present two violent patients suffering from severe Personality Disorders, illustrating the changes that can arise during the course of several years' psychoanalytic treatment in high security hospital. Progress was monitored using the Operationalised Psychodynamic Diagnostics (OPD) system. The clinical presentations will indicate how secluded these patients' minds are to themselves and to those involved in their care. The psychoanalytic treatment, as one part of the overall treatment, is described to show how the secluded parts of the patients'' minds can be approached, including references to the regular regressions that arise, provoked by ‘getting better’.

responseSet.title: Psychosis: Psychodynamic work with families
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychosis: Psychodynamic work with families

Brian Martindale and Judith Smith

Judith Smith

Sunderland, UK

Judith Smith  

Psychoanalysts suggested psychological work with families where a member has a psychosis back in the 1950s and 1960s. The paper gives a brief historical outline and then describes a number of reasons – including compatibility with NICE Guidelines – why a psychodynamic approach should be made available.

A case example is given of psychodynamic family work in an Early Intervention in Psychosis service where the father of three children had a protracted psychosis and the mother's own history had played into a lack of expectations of her husband; this couple dynamic played an important role in infantilizing the patient rather than supporting him in his role as father and husband. Psychological work that involved the mother was an essential key to supporting her husband's development and confidence with substantive benefits to all members of the family including the children.

responseSet.title: A woman's unconscious use of her body. A psychoanalytical perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A woman's unconscious use of her body. A psychoanalytical perspective

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter

London now in private practice

Joan Schachter  

Dinora Pines, London, Routledge, 2010, £19.99 (paperback)

This book, consisting of a collection of papers written in the 1970s and 1980s, was first published by Virago in 1993, and it has now been re-published having been out of print for some years. Dinora Pines considers the major events in a woman's sexual life: adolescence, pregnancy and motherhood, abortion, miscarriage and infertility, the menopause and old age. She gives detailed clinical material from analytic work, which amply illustrates her view that bodily changes inherent in these events always evoke, usually in the form of a re-enactment, problematic elements of the individual's infantile emotional development. From her early work as a GP and dermatologist, Pines came to understand that the body expressed what could not be verbalized: ‘pain that was unbearable and unthinkable’ (p. 3). She observed that bodily expressions of unbearable feelings were more common in women than in men and that ‘a mature woman's body offered a means of avoiding thought and facing psychic conflict’ (p. 4). She also recognized the emotional importance for her women patients of talking to and being touched by a woman doctor. This particular perspective of women analysts treating women patients has been further explored in two books edited by Rosine Perelberg and Joan Raphael-Leff (1997 and 2008).

The first paper ‘Skin communication’ describes Pines' understanding of the women she treated when she worked as a dermatologist; experiences which prompted her to seek psychoanalytic training. She elaborates her view about the nature of the pre-verbal trauma of infantile eczema through material from the analysis of a woman with a history of infantile eczema who presented with severe depression, with suicidal and psychotic episodes. She conveys the emergence in the transference and counter-transference of the disturbed mother-infant relationship resulting from the skin condition; in particular, the narcissistic problems in which shame and extreme sensitivity to the emotional responses of the object predominate. The analytic setting with its conditions of abstinence, provoke particular frustration in such patients often leading to acting-out.

responseSet.title: Breakdown and development – A clinical case study
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Breakdown and development – A clinical case study

Estela Gloger

Estela Gloger


London, UK

Estela Gloger  

severely disturbed patients, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, NHS services, breakdown

In this paper I will attempt to describe the treatment of a seriously ill patient in a National Health Service (NHS) clinic who suffered from recurrent depressive disorder with co-morbidity, repeated breakdowns and was at risk of suicide. The purpose of writing a clinical case study is to illustrate the vital importance of a psychoanalytically-informed approach in the treatment of a very ill patient in the public sector. At a time when there is a decrease in the provision of long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy my aim is to illustrate its value in the understanding and treatment of complex disorders, a perspective that seems to be under constant threat from internal and external pressures and at risk of being sidelined as a non-evidence-based form of treatment.

I will try to describe the different stages in the treatment; from referral through to the beginning of treatment, its interruption, the patient's breakdown and admission, and finally his return to treatment upon discharge. The ending will be discussed with a view to highlighting some of the patient's internal movements enabled by the work undertaken.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychosis as a defence against unbearable terrors: Discussant response to Antony Garelick's paper

Mary Brownescombe Heller

Mary Brownescombe Heller

UK

Mary Brownescombe Heller  

I first met Richard nearly 14 years ago. He and Brian O'Neill, both representing the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the NHS, came to a conference I had organised in Middlesbrough, Teesside, where I managed a small NHS Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Service. Richard came up from London and gave us one of his masterly papers on psychosis.

There are references in the other papers collected here to how Richard thought about psychosis, as well as his humour, kindness and humanity. I would like to add that he had enormous interest in and knowledge of football: that day he and I had a lively discussion about ‘The Boro’, Middlesbrough's football team – which was then doing well in the Premier League. Following on from the conference Richard was enormously supportive and encouraging to me and the other members of our small service and the efforts we were making to establish psychoanalytic psychotherapy as a clinical service and a way of thinking within what was then Teesside's NHS Trust.

My last memory of Richard comes from a half-day conference celebrating the work of Dr Leslie Sohn, which took place at the end of September 2007 and was held in the Institute of Psychoanalysis. Richard was speaking at this event, although clearly experiencing considerable pain after having a facial nerve operation for the re-occurence of his cancer. He was having difficulty moving one side of his face and, as a result, in articulating his words; yet despite all this, he was able to be himself, to be very present and to speak at some length – in a way that I am sure all those of us who were there felt showed his enormous courage and fortitude.

Tony's paper brought to mind thoughts of Richard and his work. Like Tony, I too first came across an example of psychosis when I was in training as a clinical psychologist. My supervisor had asked me to give a memory assessment and other cognitive tests to a middle-aged man who had apparently been complaining of memory problems. I had scarcely started on the Wechsler Memory Test with him, before he anxiously broke off to ask me why it was that other people were talking about him and telling each other things that had happened to him in his childhood; things that he himself had forgotten.

responseSet.title: Reading Winnicott/Between Winnicott and Lacan. A clinical engagement
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading Winnicott/Between Winnicott and Lacan. A clinical engagement

Geraldine Shipton

Geraldine Shipton


Geraldine Shipton  

Lesley Caldwell and Angela Joyce, London, Routledge and NLPA, 2011, 308 pp., £19.76 (paperback)

Lewis A. Kirsner, London, Routledge, 2011, 174 pp., £17.09 (paperback)

I wish that both of these books had been published some years ago when I was introducing psychotherapy trainees and psychoanalytic studies students to Winnicott's contributions to psychoanalysis. Reading Winnicott is much more than a collection of 14 key papers by Winnicott with introductions by the editors, although that alone would have been helpful. Lesley Caldwell and Angela Joyce contextualize Winnicott's evolving ideas before each selected paper with reference to contemporaneous and more recent debates, bringing out illuminating connections and resonances related to a wide variety of thinkers. For example, they identify a link between Michael Fordham's Jungian ideas about the self and solitude and Winnicott's thoughts about the necessity for isolation at a deep part of the self where experience is felt. Winnicott, however insisted it must not be an insulated kind of isolation. Numerous other connections are made, some of which are not new, like the way in which mirroring was taken up by both Lacan and Winnicott (also featured in Kirsner's book) and, throughout the text, Winnicott's debt to and departure from Kleinian ideas are clarified. The editors' selection thus enriches our general grasp of theory and in particular practice, be it related to psychotherapy, psychoanalysis or social work. This is a lucid, scholarly addition to the growing literature on Winnicott's unique perspective on psychoanalysis and on the development of a human person.

Winnicott's papers themselves are both a pleasure to read and frustrating at times. He is not a didactic writer and his thoughts are profoundly rooted in clinical practice as well as his own experiencing self. He liked to use paradox and to write in such a way that explanation is partnered with a kind of poetic evocation. Indeed, he writes rather as he played the Squiggle game – the reader needing to complete the text by bringing her own experience and thoughts to it in order for it to make sense.

responseSet.title: Interview with Dr Mark Blechner
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interview with Dr Mark Blechner

Jeremy Clarke and Mark Blechner

Mark Blechner

Mark Blechner  

Mark Blechner is Editor of a leading American psychoanalytic journal, Contemporary Psychoanalysis, and a psychoanalyst who has published important papers on a range of subjects from neuro-psychoanalysis, through panic attacks and depression, to dream interpretation and working with projective identification.1 He is also an openly gay analyst who has made a significant difference to the way the profession in the USA deals with the issue of homosexuality. His work in this area is chronicled in his recent book Sex changes (Blechner, 2009), which opens with a quote from John Cage: ‘I can't understand why people are frightened of new ideas. I'm frightened of old ones’. The interview took the form of a series of email exchanges between Dr Blechner and Jeremy Clarke.*

Sexuality and criteria for mental health

JC: I want to start with a phrase used by Muriel Dimen in a remarkable paper that you have just published (Dimen, 2010) about sexual violation in an analytic treatment. She writes: ‘I was 26 and it was 1968, an era of political, personal, cultural and intellectual change, but in which women's sexual subjectivity was still officially less than their own’ (p. 35). Can we explore this idea that sexual subjectivity can be something ‘officially’ less than one's own because it is something you have also written about in your book, Sex changes (Blechner, 2009). What I am interested in is this idea of an ‘official’ space within which sexuality functions and that this, in some important way, is part of what determines who we are allowed to be in society both at the level of personal sexual and mental wellbeing and in relation to what we might call a collective unconscious.

MJB: The ‘official’ space of sexuality is, I presume, in the public world at large. Virtually all societies prescribe and proscribe certain forms of sexuality. Individuals may subjectively experience their sexuality in one way, but learn to keep that sexuality more or less hidden to evade social judgement or punishment. That is the origin of gay people being in the closet, but probably no one's sexuality entirely fits the rules of society, also sometimes called ‘normalcy’. The official view of sexuality is in constant flux–a continual dialectic, between hiding and being open, between oppression and liberation. The flux is not all in one direction; progress toward greater freedom is, from time to time, thwarted by a retrogressive step toward suppression.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma  

This issue of the Journal is a ‘special’ issue not because it focuses exclusively on a particular topic, but because it is dedicated to a very special colleague, Richard Lucas, OBE, who died on 28 July 2008. Some of the papers (by Evans, Garelick, Heller, Minne, Steiner and Harland, and Sohn) are modified versions of papers given at a conference that celebrated Richard's work and was organized by the Institute of Psychoanalysis and supported by the APP and the Royal College of Psychiatrists – organizations that Richard was involved in during his career. We are also pleased to include other papers in this issue that were not presented at this conference, but which resonate with the kind of application of psychoanalytic ideas that I think Richard would have enthusiastically supported and engaged with (the papers by Ivey, Darnley et al., and Martindale and Smith).

Throughout his career, Richard was passionate about, and committed to, the application of psychoanalysis in general psychiatry in the NHS. He was one of the founding members of the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy (APP) and supportive of this Journal. He made seminal contributions to work with psychotic patients, introducing the understanding of the ‘psychotic wavelength’, as well as papers on puerperal and manic depressive states. Richard's unwavering belief that psychoanalysis had a vital contribution to make to the care of psychotic patients in particular is unfortunately not reflected in the NICE recommendations for schizophrenia.

Liz Kuipers, who chaired the NICE Guideline Development Group for schizophrenia, has written a very helpful overview for this Journal of the state of play in this domain. Although these guidelines make no reference to psychoanalytic interventions, reading the papers in this issue only serves to emphasize the importance of keeping alive the contribution that psychoanalytic ideas can make to clinical work in this field, not least in helping the staff caring for psychotic patients to manage the impact on them of this work and so remain alive to the meaning in psychotic presentations.

responseSet.title: On the relations between autism and psychoanalytic thought and practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the relations between autism and psychoanalytic thought and practice

R. Hobson

R. Hobson

London, UK

R. Hobson  

autism, intersubjectivity, psychoanalysis

I consider the nature of the psychoanalytic enterprise, both methodologically and conceptually, and the various ways in which it may bear upon, and be affected by considerations from, individuals with autism. I conclude that in certain respects, phenomena from autism may serve to highlight the value of psychoanalytic concepts, precisely because those concepts are not applicable to children with autism in quite the way that they are to other human beings. Psychoanalytic writers and practitioners need to be especially circumspect when extending their customary ways of thinking and working to individuals with autism. The potential value and limitations of dynamic psychotherapy of some kind with affected children and adults, as well as with their families, are little understood.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The internet and its role in the escalation of sexually compulsive behaviour

Heather Wood

Heather Wood


London,

Heather Wood  

internet sex, internet pornography, child pornography, internet offenders, escalation

As increasing numbers of patients present with problems of compulsive use of internet sex and internet pornography, psychotherapists are challenged to develop models that address the powerful, and potentially disturbing effect of this stimulus. Internet sex does not just facilitate access to sexual materials but can act as a catalyst, affecting established defences and ego and superego functioning. Escalation in sexually compulsive behaviour, which affects a small proportion of those accessing online sex, may be thought of as a series of transitions: from casual to compulsive use, from compulsive use to illegal use, and from use of illegal pornography to contact offending. Drawing on clinical experience and the available research evidence, psychodynamic processes associated with movement through these transitions are considered. Internet sex has the potential to fuel manic defences, invites narcissistic and part-object relating, provides a vehicle for the expression and disowning of sadistic impulses and can potentially undermine superego functioning. The combined effect of these processes may lead to a breakdown of ego controls in vulnerable individuals.

responseSet.title: The ‘autistic’ level of the Oedipus complex
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The ‘autistic’ level of the Oedipus complex

Maria Rhode

Maria Rhode

London, UK

Maria Rhode  

autism, primitive Oedipus complex, annihilation anxiety, sense perception, psychic skin

In this paper, the author describes some of the tripartite constellations occurring in the material of children on the autistic spectrum. These could be thought of as constituting a primitive, ‘autistic’ level of the Oedipus complex where the fundamental anxieties are existential ones. It is suggested that these fears of annihilation are experienced on a bodily level, and that this physical aspect appears to be a distinguishing feature of the experience of the Oedipal constellation in children with autism. The clinical illustrations concern the experience of bodily mutilation within the feeding relationship; Oedipal aspects of sense perception; the nature of the link between masculine and feminine elements making up the psychic skin; and the balance between the child, the mother, and the mother' internal figures. Some implications for cognitive development are discussed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex in psychotherapy: Sexuality, passion, love, and desire in the therapeutic encounter

William Edkins

William Edkins


William Edkins  

Lawrence Hedges, Routledge, 2011, 202 pp, £18.99

In his book, Sex in psychotherapy: Sexuality, passion, love and desire in the therapeutic encounter, Hedges is directly challenging accepted myths and preconceptions that distort our understanding of sex, gender and the erotic. He specifically focuses on psychoanalytic technique and the limited ways in which we, as analysts and therapists, think about sexuality in our consulting rooms, especially within ourselves in reaction to our patients. He argues, convincingly, that there is a need to actively engage our patients in the erotic transference/counter-transference.

To help the reader to rethink this whole area, Hedges utilizes discoveries over the past few decades from a comprehensive spectrum of fields of knowledge ‘that hold revolutionary implications for psychotherapeutic approaches to sex and sexuality’ (p. xv). It is as if Hedges is going back to late nineteenth century Vienna and taking a second look at libido and related psychoanalytic technique through the lens of what we now know and how we now think in the early twentieth century.

In the Introduction and Part 1 of the book Hedges lists and discusses the perspectives and discoveries that have changed the way we currently view sexuality. Some of those mentioned include: the historical and cross-cultural perspectives; brain and neurological research; early attachment and internalized erotic interaction patterns; dissociation and multiple selves perspective; trauma and transgenerational ghosts; and intersubjective and the relational third perspectives.

From this ‘revisit’, Hedges confirms and expands on Freud's discoveries of the complexities of sex and gender. Other implications he notes challenge or even contradict Freud's understanding of the role of the analyst's erotic responses to his or her patients and lead Hedges to propose the need to directly share and utilize these counter-transference experiences.

He then ties these findings together in what has culminated for him as ‘listening perspectives’. The listening perspective approach is spelled out in detail in his prior book, Listening perspectives in psychotherapy (1983). I think it would have been helpful for readers who are not familiar with this previous book for Hedges to elaborate more on what he means by these perspectives. Basically, listening perspectives is Hedges' model of development, integrating his own clinical discoveries with Mahler's stages and Kohut's theory on the development of the self. In order of development these are: the personality in organization; symbiosis and separation; the emergent self in self-other experience; and self and other constancy in independent experience.

responseSet.title: Pinned against the ropes: Understanding anti-social personality-disordered patients through use of the counter-transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pinned against the ropes: Understanding anti-social personality-disordered patients through use of the counter-transference

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans


London,

Marcus Evans  

anti-social, personality disorder, supervision, psychoanalysis, forensic

This paper focuses on difficulties in the treatment and care of patients with a diagnosis of psychopathic, or anti-social personality disorder as it is usually referred to today. The current psychiatric diagnostic systems (ICD-10 and DSM-IV) are primarily categorical and theoretical. Diagnosis is arrived at by assessing the presence or absence of symptoms and behaviours. These diagnostic systems do not take any account of the individual's development or personality structure and consequently there is no dimension for describing links between different diagnoses. This means that these patients are often thought to be either suffering from paranoid schizophrenia or anti-social personality disorder or a dual diagnosis depending on the phase of their illness. The current diagnostic system does not recognize the underlying personality structures that link the diagnosis and cycles of the illness together. However DSM-V, which is coming out next year, will take a more dimensional approach, and as such will be more compatible with a psychoanalytic understanding of mental disorder.

In this paper the author draws upon literature in this area and suggests that this dichotomy between the psychotic and the anti-social may be too simple and argues that if we can understand that psychotic processes may well also underlie the personality disorders, we may reach a better understanding of what takes place in these settings and how staff are affected by the patients. In order to function effectively, staff need regular, on-going supervision. These arguments will be illustrated with clinical examples taken from supervision groups and consultations in forensic and mental health settings.

responseSet.title: Disrupting Oedipus: The legacy of the Sphinx
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disrupting Oedipus: The legacy of the Sphinx

Nicola Barden

Nicola Barden


UK

Nicola Barden  

psychoanalysis, gender, sexuality, homosexuality, Oedipus

This paper aims to trace the legacy of the Oedipal foundations on analytic theories of gender and sexuality. Hetero-normativity places restrictions not only on homosexuality but also on gender and heterosexuality and these drawbacks have failed to be adequately recognized, indeed have been safeguarded at considerable cost to lived lives of all genders and sexualities.

Traditional approaches to homosexuality have viewed it as a gender disorder, a result of cross-gender identification. This view persists, albeit in more sophisticated form, as an inevitable result of the barrier between identification and desire that is crucial to the Oedipal resolution. Re-theorizing homosexuality is necessary to free gender from its sexual constrictions; re-theorizing gender is necessary to free homosexuality from its gendered constrictions; both require re-theorizing Oedipus.

A foundation for such challenge, however, was laid by Freud himself in his recognition of the impossibility of defining gendered content, while necessarily making attempts to do so, and his conviction that homosexuality was no illness, while being unable to save it from pathology. The continual tying of the homosexuality/gender knot does a disservice to the radical origins of the psychoanalytic tradition and an even greater disservice to the further understanding of sexuality and gender.

responseSet.title: Lesbian and gay couple relationships: When internalized homophobia gets in the way of couple creativity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lesbian and gay couple relationships: When internalized homophobia gets in the way of couple creativity

Leezah Hertzmann

Leezah Hertzmann

London, UK

Leezah Hertzmann  

lesbian and gay, couple relationship, internalized homophobia, couple superego, unconscious beliefs, Oedipus complex

Many lesbian and gay couples remain cautious in seeking psychoanalytic treatment for their relationship difficulties. The use of psychoanalytic theories, particularly the application of the Oedipus complex to explore homosexuality has been experienced by many as especially damaging. It is regrettable that psychoanalytic theory has too often been used in a restrictive and deterministic way to pathologize sexual orientation. Yet, the concept of the Oedipus complex has also been immensely fruitful for psychoanalysis and remains one of its central tenets. In this paper I use the concept of the Creative Couple, an idea which has evolved from the Oedipus complex, to underpin my thinking in an exploration of some of the issues with which I have observed lesbian and gay couples struggle. One such issue is that of Internalized Homophobia. Using case examples I consider the damage this can cause to lesbian and gay couples, including the sense of paralysis that can pervade both the couple's relationship and the therapy. I reflect on the challenges that internalized homophobia can present for the therapist when manifested in the transference and countertransference, and the need to be aware of the hidden, pernicious ways in which it can interfere with a couple's creativity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The queer relationship between psychoanalysts and their gay and lesbian patients

Michael King

Michael King


London, UK

Michael King  

homosexuality, psychotherapy, psychoanalysis, therapist sexual orientation

Throughout most of the twentieth century psychoanalysis regarded same-sex desire as a pathological or arrested condition. As people became less hostile to homosexuality in the late twentieth century, so did the psychoanalysts, only more slowly. In this paper the author briefly traces the evolution of psychoanalytic ideas on homosexuality, before examining how today's psychoanalytical practitioners and their patients regard treatment of same-sex desire, focusing mainly on research conducted over the past 15 years.

Antony Garelick  

psychotic states, psychoanalytic technique, container, primitive defences

Mental mechanisms and issues of psychoanalytic technique in the treatment of psychotic states are explored and illustrated with clinical vignettes. The value of establishing a psychosis workshop to facilitate understanding and an appropriate language to communicate with the patient drawing on the work of Dr Richard Lucas and the author is described.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotic processes in forensic institutions

Brian Darnley , Ronald Doctor , John Gordon and Gabriel Kirtchuk

Gabriel Kirtchuk

Middlesex
UK

Gabriel Kirtchuk  

In this paper the authors illustrate how they have developed the contributions made by Richard Lucas towards the psychoanalytic understanding of psychosis and in particular his use of the concepts of psychotic and non-psychotic parts of the personality. They discuss how they have developed these ideas in their own work within forensic institutions where the psychotic part of the personality can be so in the ascendancy that little communication is possible with the non-psychotic part of the patient. These situations are often beyond words, and containment might have to involve limit-setting in the form of physical boundaries and the use of seclusion. It is our intention to show how meaning can be created for staff even when this is beyond the patients' grasp; and how, through the use of reflective practice, we can help staff to recover their equilibrium and move towards a more integrated picture of the patient. This allows staff to become more available to their patients as containing objects.

responseSet.title: The role of psychoanalytic assessment in the management and care of a psychotic patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The role of psychoanalytic assessment in the management and care of a psychotic patient

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

London, UK

Marcus Evans  

The pressure on mental health services to move patients on quickly, and the emphasis on the recovery model, can encourage a superficial assessment of the patient's difficulties. The author argues that a narrow definition of the medical model based on the presence or absence of symptoms ignores the deeper psychic structures that govern thinking and behaviour over time. A psychoanalytic assessment can offer a dynamic picture of deeper psychic structures in the patient's internal world. This is particularly important with psychotic patients who may successfully use denial and rationalization to cover up their underlying psychopathology. The developmental perspective inherent in the psychoanalytic view can also give a picture of the patient's propensity to repeat certain aspects of his/her psychopathology. In addition, a psychoanalytic approach may shine some light on the relationship between relapse and risk behaviour.

responseSet.title: Invasive objects: Minds under siege
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Invasive objects: Minds under siege

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale


Northumberland,


Sunderland,

Brian Martindale  

Paul Williams, London, Routledge, 2010, 252 pp.

This book is mostly a collection of the previous publications of the British psychoanalyst and anthropologist Paul Williams. A couple of chapters were written in conjunction with Murray Jackson, both authors having a long-standing interest in psychosis. Since the book is a compilation, each chapter can be enjoyed in its own right. There is however a leitmotif running throughout much of the book: the increasing evidence that contemporary psychoanalysis can make mutative changes in complex pathologically structured aspects of the mind that were laid down in the earliest history of the individual. These basic structures permeate the daily struggles of those adults who come for psychoanalysis as a result of constriction in their lives and their capacities in human relationships. The case material in this book is therefore connected with the psychoanalysis of very early disturbances in the sense of self, with accompanying theoretical explanations, and the author describes the challenges to technique and underlines the emotional demands on both psychoanalyst and analysand for this work to be sustained.

There were a number of chapters that I found myself particularly enthralled by. One chapter that has not been published before is about John Nash. Many readers will be familiar with A beautiful mind: the name of both a popular film and a biography that depict Nash's life including his very prolonged schizophrenic breakdown followed by recovery. Some regarded Nash's mathematical/economic theoretical advances as the greatest of the last century and indeed he was awarded the Nobel prize in 1994 for discoveries in economics made prior to his breakdown. Williams and Jackson, based on extensive information about Nash available in the public sphere, attempt a psychoanalytic understanding of Nash's psychosis and recovery and his pre-morbid vulnerability. Especially interesting are their psychoanalytic hypotheses on the remarkable observation that his recovery after decades of psychosis (eventually without the aid of medication) was accompanied by a welcome development from his pre-morbid personality indicated by an increased capacity for both concern for and appreciation of others compared with his previous narcissism. Nash's prolonged but successful struggle and recovery from the relentless propaganda from his merciless superego dissociated in the form of hallucinations is touchingly linked with Coleridge's poem The rime of the ancient mariner and the shedding of the Mariner's albatross.
